Avidly he kept an eye on the weather to catch Mughal ruins and the modern flats they sat beside. He celebrated humped Zebu cattle and the texture of churned-up soil. Two old men walking in opposite a railway station standing stock still, reading their newspapers, while of the world, but when he was invited to join the Magnum agency in inside his country’s many layers. He could have been a photographer Only an Indian could do this, someone who understood from the was led not by his mind, but by his heart.

beating whole, seeing them with a pure and unsentimental eye that that. Humbly he preferred to merge with his people, a part of the raising their arms, did not try to pose for him, and he did not want boy splashing paint and running off laughing, mourning women near-naked wrestlers relaxing under Kolkata’s Howrah bridge, the years he had learned patience. Mud-sculptors making goddesses, and he carried no bags. His movements were leisurely. Over the Z8 usually his faithful Nikon  with a zoom lens, hung round his neck, energy. He was not an intrusive photographer. Only one camera, there was barely a frame that did not show their intensity and enchanted him. Throughout his more than 18 books of photo essays, India in every aspect was his canvas, but it was the people who alone could pick up the truth and ensure it lived for ever.

the frame. Yet he had a responsibility to do so, for photography know how to record something as huge as this; too much lay outside around him. His own feelings were close to suffocation.
